definition: Cross-currency basis widens during dollar funding stress and functions as a valuable real-time stress indicator (7/10), but not a reliable early-warning predictor (5/10). With ~5% crisis base rate and ~15-20% false alarm rate, positive predictive value is only 15-25%. Portfolio thresholds (-60bp JPY, -35bp EUR) are useful but should not be the primary risk management tool.
evidence: Mechanistic explanation sound: dollar funding stress manifests in basis swaps before broader markets
Bayesian critique: positive predictive value cannot exceed ~25% even if signal is genuinely informative
No ROC curve, precision-recall analysis, or false positive rate provided
Useful portfolio thresholds: -60bp JPY / -35bp EUR triggers
open_questions: What is the proper false-positive framework for basis widening as a crisis indicator
Whether the early-warning window is genuinely shrinking or shock heterogeneity explains apparent compression
Whether decomposition into regulatory/credit/residual components improves signal quality
